persons.arguments_against: The strongest counter-case: a same-name relative (Nevill is fairly common -- 12 officials carry it) with the identical two initials 'J. T.' could in theory share the colony. But that hypothesis must also account for the identical, singular compound office: 'Inspector of lighthouses and superintendent of public buildings, Newfoundland' (position_sim 100). This is a unique colonial post held by one person at a time, and the staff list shows it held continuously and unchanged by J. T. Nevill from 1877 to 1897. The bio's two initials (not a single initial, so the namesake trap is weaker) match exactly; the 1874 appointment precedes the 1877 first listing consistently; and the 1886 edition shows the identical name+position. A relative holding the identical singular office under the identical initials is not credible. The objection fails.

evidence_cited.fact: J. T. Nevill | Inspector of Lighthouses and Superintendent of Public Buildings
Inspector of Lighthouses and Superintendent of Public Buildings

persons.would_overturn: A printed record of a second J. T. Nevill holding the same Newfoundland lighthouse/public-buildings office, or a death date for the bio incompatible with continued listing through 1897.

notes: Two-initial match (J. T.), unique compound office held continuously 1877-1897, colony agreement, date consistency, and same-edition (1886) co-occurrence with position_sim 100. No live counter-hypothesis.
